---
title: With BLoC
description: Use Qora for server-state and BLoC for UI state machines — each tool doing what it does best.
---

BLoC is excellent at modelling complex UI flows as explicit state machines — multi-step checkout, authentication, form wizards. It is less well-suited to the repetitive cycle of fetching server data, caching it, retrying on failure, and revalidating in the background.

Adding Qora to a BLoC project means **specialising your stack**: BLoC stays in charge of UI state machines, Qora takes over server-state management.

---

## Division of Labour

| Responsibility                                  | BLoC                         | Qora                                   |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Fetch user profile                              |                              | `QoraBuilder`                          |
| Fetch product list / infinite feed              |                              | `QoraBuilder` / `InfiniteQueryBuilder` |
| Create, update, delete (mutations)              |                              | `QoraMutationBuilder`                  |
| Multi-step checkout flow                        | `CheckoutBloc`               |                                        |
| Form validation state                           | BLoC or `ValueNotifier`      |                                        |
| Authentication flow                             | `AuthBloc`                   |                                        |
| Optimistic UI + cache invalidation after write  |                              | `setQueryData` + `invalidate`          |

The two coexist cleanly: `QoraScope` nests inside the `BlocProvider` tree (or vice versa).

---

## The Anti-Pattern: Data-Fetching BLoC

A BLoC that only wraps an API call provides no structural benefit over a function call, yet carries the full overhead of events, states, and wiring:

```dart [lib/blocs/user_bloc.dart]
abstract class UserEvent {}
class LoadUser extends UserEvent { final int id; LoadUser(this.id); }

abstract class UserState {}
class UserInitial   extends UserState {}
class UserLoading   extends UserState {}
class UserLoaded    extends UserState { final User user; UserLoaded(this.user); }
class UserError     extends UserState { final String message; UserError(this.message); }

class UserBloc extends Bloc<UserEvent, UserState> {
  final UserApi _api;

  UserBloc(this._api) : super(UserInitial()) {
    on<LoadUser>((event, emit) async {
      emit(UserLoading());
      try {
        emit(UserLoaded(await _api.getUser(event.id)));
      } catch (e) {
        emit(UserError('$e')); // no retry, no deduplication, no SWR
      }
    });
  }
}
```

```dart [lib/user_screen.dart]
BlocBuilder<UserBloc, UserState>(
  builder: (context, state) => switch (state) {
    UserLoading()             => const CircularProgressIndicator(),
    UserLoaded(:final user)   => UserCard(user),
    UserError(:final message) => ErrorWidget(message),
    _                         => const SizedBox.shrink(),
  },
)
```

---

## After: QoraBuilder for Server Data

```dart [lib/user_screen.dart]
QoraBuilder<User>(
  queryKey: ['users', userId],
  queryFn: () => UserApi.getUser(userId),
  options: const QoraOptions(staleTime: Duration(minutes: 5)),
  builder: (context, state, fetchStatus) => switch (state) {
    Success(:final data) => UserCard(data),
    Loading()            => const CircularProgressIndicator(),
    Failure(:final error)=> ErrorWidget('$error'),
    Initial()            => const SizedBox.shrink(),
  },
)
```

No bloc, no events, no manual transitions. Retry with backoff, request deduplication, and background revalidation on app focus and reconnect are all built in.

The third builder argument `fetchStatus` distinguishes `FetchStatus.fetching` (active request) from `FetchStatus.paused` (offline, waiting for reconnect).

---

## BLoC + Qora on the Same Screen

Complex screens often need both: a UI state machine (BLoC) driving a view that also displays server data (Qora). They compose without friction:

```dart [lib/checkout_screen.dart]
BlocBuilder<CheckoutBloc, CheckoutState>(
  builder: (context, checkoutState) {
    // CheckoutBloc drives the step machine
    if (checkoutState is SelectingAddress) {
      return QoraBuilder<List<Address>>(
        queryKey: ['users', userId, 'addresses'],
        queryFn: () => api.getAddresses(userId),
        builder: (context, state, fetchStatus) => switch (state) {
          Success(:final data) => AddressSelector(
              addresses: data,
              onSelect: (address) =>
                  context.read<CheckoutBloc>().add(AddressSelected(address)),
            ),
          Loading()            => const CircularProgressIndicator(),
          Failure(:final error)=> ErrorWidget('$error'),
          Initial()            => const SizedBox.shrink(),
        },
      );
    }
    if (checkoutState is ReviewingOrder) {
      return OrderReviewWidget(order: checkoutState.order);
    }
    return const SizedBox.shrink();
  },
)
```

BLoC controls *which step* is shown. Qora provides the server data *within* each step.

---

## Invalidating Qora from a BLoC

When a BLoC performs a write (a form submission, a completed checkout step), it can invalidate Qora's cache so the next read gets fresh data:

```dart [lib/blocs/update_user_bloc.dart]
class UpdateUserBloc extends Bloc<UpdateUserEvent, UpdateUserState> {
  final QoraClient _client;
  final UserApi _api;

  UpdateUserBloc(this._client, this._api) : super(UpdateUserInitial()) {
    on<SubmitUpdate>((event, emit) async {
      emit(UpdateUserLoading());
      try {
        await _api.updateUser(event.id, event.name);
        _client.invalidate(['users', event.id]); // Qora refetches on next read
        emit(UpdateUserSuccess());
      } catch (e) {
        emit(UpdateUserError('$e'));
      }
    });
  }
}
```

Pass `QoraClient` into BLoCs that need to coordinate with the server-state cache. Do not pass `BuildContext` — the client is a shared cache object, not a widget concern.

---

## Setup

```dart [lib/main.dart]
final qoraClient = QoraClient(
  config: const QoraClientConfig(debugMode: kDebugMode),
);

runApp(
  MultiBlocProvider(
    providers: [
      BlocProvider(create: (_) => AuthBloc()),
      BlocProvider(create: (ctx) => CheckoutBloc(ctx.read())),
    ],
    child: QoraScope(
      client: qoraClient,
      lifecycleManager: FlutterLifecycleManager(qoraClient: qoraClient),
      connectivityManager: FlutterConnectivityManager(),
      child: const MyApp(),
    ),
  ),
);
```

`QoraScope` can nest inside `MultiBlocProvider` or outside — both work. Choose whichever reads more clearly in your app.

---

## Checklist

- [ ] Add `flutter_qora` to `pubspec.yaml`
- [ ] Add `QoraScope` to the widget tree alongside `MultiBlocProvider`
- [ ] Identify BLoCs that only do `emit(Loading) → fetch → emit(Loaded/Error)` — replace with `QoraBuilder`
- [ ] Keep BLoCs with multiple events, complex transitions, or non-fetch business logic
- [ ] Remove `BlocProvider` for data-only blocs
- [ ] Replace `BlocBuilder` for data-only blocs with `QoraBuilder`
- [ ] Inject `QoraClient` into BLoCs that perform writes and need to invalidate the cache

---
